speaker: interviewer
text: When we were talking about your father getting his beard cut by the Germans, did he feel that, religiously, something bad was done to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he felt violated. The beard was whole life. By Jews, religious Jews, you must, you cannot use a razor blade, you cannot. So he knew he was violated. It was without his consent, but he was concerned. Let me tell you an incident that happened-- just talking about it, I remember. I think it was the first week, also-- you know, right away in the beginning, they let us know who they are and what they stand for. That's why there was no doubt in anybody's mind what they were capable of doing. The excuse was, they were looking for weapons. So of course, they search. We were not military people, that we had weapons. So we had nothing even to hide. They came to our house. And we used to have closets in Europe. It wasn't here, like a closet is hidden.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was open, an extra. A [NON-ENGLISH] we used to call it. They opened up, threw out the clothing, took out the bag from my father-- a tallis and [? tefillin. ?] It was in a bag. And they said, was ist das? So my father says, [NON-ENGLISH]-- to pray.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took it, out through the window. Out, we were on the first floor, living on the first floor. My father saw it, and he is walking toward the window. He starts running after him, and he wanted, like almost, to dive, jump out after that, but my mother stopped him. Jewish articles, a tallis and tefillin, should never touch the floor. This is like committing a crime. And my father felt he was violated very hard. And they were laughing. By the way, at that time, things changed in me regarding religion. I want to make that clear, because I'm not, now, a religious man. Since that time, I start turning over to the other way. I never returned to religion, because they were laughing. The Germans were laughing from us.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, they had, on their buckles, the belt buckles-- Gott ist mit uns-- God is with us. That's what they had. And they were laughing. When he threw out, and saw that my father was emotional-- wanted to jump after that, out the window-- he said, wo ist eur Gott? Where is your God, now? So I saw he was right, and I was wrong, in my opinion, of course. People wouldn't think so. People would think differently-- just to bring out the point, what happened. And then, this is early in their occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very early.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did things go after that with their occupation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worse and worse. Every day, another notch was pulled on the belt. We heard rumors that there was going to be a ghetto. Certainly, one day, they started putting in big timber in the ground and made fences, fenced off a certain area, but it wasn't closed yet. It was fenced off, and doors were left-- wide doors, like a whole street, the width of a street-- open, so people can go, horse and buggies can go, you know, travel back and forth. However, there came a time that was in April-- April or, maybe, May, of 1940-- that the ghetto was shut. It was done during the night. These gates that were open were closed. Soldiers, German guards, were standing around in the ghetto, around the ghetto, and we could not go out. Whatever food what was there was slowly exhausted, and we were, like, nearly starving. Anyway, we created before-- we, it was created, like, by itself-- the Jewish intelligence. When the Germans came, they wanted to talk somebody, make somebody responsible for something.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they came to the Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a Jewish organization. And they asked, wo ist die alteste from the Juden? Who is the oldest from the Jews? So there was this guy, Mordechai Chaim Rumkowski. He stood up, and he said, I am. They said, good. Then, whatever we'll need from now on, we'll come to you, and you will have to supply us with that. He said, I can't promise, but I'll do my best.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said, you promise, or it's going to be the end of you. So he saw where he stands. So it means whenever he gets an order, he has to fulfill. Lots of people would blame Rumkowski for things he did, but there was no choice in the matter, unless he wanted to be killed, himself. And in fact, in certain ghettos, elsewhere, people disobeyed the order, and they disappear. So it was obvious what the outcome could be by disobeying.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after the ghetto was closed, how did you get food? What did you do to get food, or what you needed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Exactly, exactly. After the ghetto was closed, the Germans came-- excuse me-- the Germans came, and they said, we need workers. Or maybe he suggested, you know, we can open up shops, work for you, supply you with stuff that you need during the war. We have a lot of tailors in Lodz. We have shoemakers. We could supply you. You supply the goods, the raw material, we'll have shoes for you, we'll have uniforms, or hats. Whatever you need, we have the people capable of doing it. However, we want to be rewarded, not with money, with food. And somehow, he worked out with them a deal-- I don't know exactly how-- but they used to send in, regularly, food. And Rumkowski created, in the ghetto, Jewish money. The workers who worked in these resorts, whether it was a tailor resort, a shoe resort, or, or carpentry-- we used to make, for the Germans, furniture. We used to make boxes for ammunition. And so they were paid with ghetto money. And the food that they sent in, he opened up stores, and they gave out ration cards, and so and so much. But it was a diet-- it was a starvation diet, that's what. The food that they sent in per person was not enough to, to fill your stomach-- meaning, throughout the four years that I was in the Lodz ghetto, I didn't have one day that I should say I was full. And for four years, I was hungry-- every day, every week, every month, for four years, I was hungry.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your brothers and your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Identical-- my parents is a different question. My mother and my brother-- because one of my oldest brothers, Jacob, he got married around 1937, so he wasn't with us anymore. Also, it was said in the First World War, it was better to be in a smaller town than the big city. Big cities used to be bombarded.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had an inheritance in Krasnik Lubelski, what I mentioned, where my mother came from. My father said, let's go to that town, it's a small town. We wouldn't be bombarded, hopefully. It used to be easier-- we could go closer to farmers, maybe buy some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father went there-- I went there, on the same wagon-- horse and buggy, no train. It was quite a few days a ride to get there. And my mother, and one brother were left behind-- the next one to me-- Lieb-- he should tend the store and watch the apartment. Well, we are talking, now, before the ghetto was created. Mind you, I'm going back, because this incident-- I just, where you asked me, so I'm going back. That was before the ghetto was closed. I want to make it clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Lodz, I told you before, was an industrial city-- textile. And we could get things in Lodz. It wasn't official, it was more like a black market. But things that weren't available there in Krasnik Lubelski, where we went. So an older cousin of mine, who was also a relative from this area, said, let's go to Lodz, bring some stuff and sell it here. We could buy it, let's say, for one-fifth the price-- but the going price that was there, you could buy it, because we had the let's say, the resources, so much.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had the people-- we knew the people to go to. And we went, we took yarn that you need to sew in rolls. It wasn't on little spools, but it was big rolls. We put it on our body, a few. And we traveled back. And it was very rewarding, because we made money, and we could buy food on the black market. So it was very good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So one time, my mother and I-- when my mother said, I do it with my cousin-- said, how about if I go, too? We could double. It's a good thing. We know where to buy it. People from there don't know where to get the outlets, where to get it. And we did that for awhile.

speaker: interviewee
text: However, we didn't know when they were going to close the ghetto. So one time we went, and my mother and I were stuck in the ghetto and couldn't get back out. So my father was left there with other relatives. So we were separated, couldn't get there no more. So that's how my father wound up in Krasnik, and my mother and my brother, [? Leib, ?] and I were left in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your older brother that was married?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was there in Krasnik with his in-laws, because he married a cousin of mine. It's like, you've got the name on the list I gave you. My uncle, they had seven beautiful daughters and one son. And we, a lot of Jewish people, intermarried between cousins and cousins. And he married, and they went there. They rented a whole house, because one that we had as inheritance wasn't enough for everybody, because you've got quite a list of people we had. So he rented-- it was a well-off uncle, also-- Uncle Falek. And he rented a house, and all the in-laws with the married daughters-- in fact, all were married, because the last one, Gucia, got married during the war. Then it was already a civil ceremony-- quiet, nothing elaborate. Because when I remember, when my brother got married, it was in a real hall, compared to the United States. It was a beautiful wedding, pictures and everything. But the last, that was the youngest daughter, she got married, too, because they didn't want a boy and girl get together, so they married them off at the civil ceremony-- a rabbi, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, the ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your mother and your younger brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, your older brother--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, he was middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Middle brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Middle, that's correct. You've got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: They-- you were in the ghetto. What happened to your mother and your brother that were in the small town? And then I want to know what happened to you and your mother.

speaker: interviewee
text: The brother in the small town, we only got one letter from there to-- we are well, we are doing good-- and no more. And we didn't know anything from them till after the war, when I met people from that city, and I asked them. And I told them, you know-- people who survived, and they said, we remember a house there rented by-- bad, bad stories. Bad stories, very bad stories.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want to wait until you tell us what happened during the war and after the war to tell us that story? I don't want to forget.

speaker: interviewee
text: I could start telling now from-- this is what I heard, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were separated, and no way we could get together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke to people after the war, and they remember, they asked the Borensteins. They got my whole family left, I said, from Lodz to there, and they rented a house. And I told them, on the [INAUDIBLE], because I was there, too. I travelled back and forth a few trips, but then I was stuck. But I was there, so I learned already who was where. And they said, yes, there was the Germans once came and took everybody, all the Jews on the market, an open market. And one cousin of mine, female-- that uncle's daughter, who married a fellow by the name of Yehiel Feingold-- They said, women on one side and men on the other side. And this was the prettiest girl that you could see. And the German officer said to her, du, kommen hier. And he started dragging her. She ripped out her arm, went over to the men's side, and grabbed her husband around the neck. The Germans shot her from the back, dead, right then and there. I know, my brother-- what I told you, Jacob, they took him to a camp to work. That was all things that I heard after the war. It happened early in 1940, it happened-- took him to a camp to work. I have never seen him since. And this particular cousin, two of her other sisters, I don't know what happened, because they all lived in one house when they told them to get out to the market. That's where, from this one, things where remembered, what people told me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in the ghetto. What happened to you and your brother and your mother in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, remember, I told you before, Rumkowski offered to supply work product to the Germans, or the Germans asked-- either way, there were established resorts. So we got assigned jobs. They assigned me and my mother to a job where clothes from people came back on trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know what people's clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yeah, yeah. It came back, we had to sort out these clothes-- shirts to shirts, jackets to jackets, slacks to slacks, overcoats to overcoats. And some of the clothes were recognized from people what were in the ghetto-- meaning, if they took out a transport, this was the clothes from these people, And some were bullet-ridden and bloody.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we got an idea what's happening to the people what left. You know, obviously, they were killed. Lot of clothes came in, a lot of clothes that we had constantly work, in that particular resort, we separated.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was older from me, [INAUDIBLE]-- I had an uncle, Avrumci, which I marked on your list. He was a very good bricklayer. In fact, very few Jews, before the war, were bricklayers. Somehow, I don't know how-- he was my uncle, I don't know how he got this trade-- but he is a master, was a master, rather, in that trade. And in the ghetto, they needed people-- the thing, because there were some bombed-out houses from the war. And they created a building department. I remember the head was [INAUDIBLE]. [? Goodman. ?] And my uncle went, registered himself-- he's a bricklayer. Fine, we could use people. And his sons-- meaning my cousins, he introduced as his helpers, also bricklayers, and they did work in the building department.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since my brother was a watchmaker-- who needed watches? They didn't need watches. So he worked with my uncle. However, there came a time when the Germans got wise-- like, you have the stories now going on with the gold to Switzerland-- that they opened up Rumkowski [NON-ENGLISH]-- in Yiddish, it's called [NON-ENGLISH]. What is the [NON-ENGLISH]? It was like a [NON-ENGLISH], where you trade things. So everybody could bring in their gold and they would give them money-- not what is on the market, but, like, a black market price so they could afford to buy extra food. So my brother's a watchmaker, and knowingly in gold, could test the gold, because the Germans gave them all these chemicals that needed-- remember, he used to scratch it, put on some of the stuff-- I don't remember what it's called-- but it was a chemical and could tell whether it was gold. So he got a job. So at least-- because he used to carry, there, two pails of cement. I want you to know, cement is very heavy. Because I tried that, you know, a few times, to help to make a few bucks extra, when I had a chance. It nearly pulled my arms off my socket pits. That's how heavy. And he worked that eight, nine, or ten hours a day. So my mother was relieved when he got the shop in the [NON-ENGLISH] as testing gold or fixing watches, repair watches for the Germans. Because, eventually, this gold was not for the Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: Rumkowski get it there in the [NON-ENGLISH], and when they had enough, they called up the Germans, told them, come pick up-- I don't know, 10 kilo, 20 kilo, or whatever. And they came, and that was, already, a good job, there. However, the food rations did not increase. So you had to do work, and sometimes hard work, and with a low diet-- very little food. It wasn't easy. Whoever survived-- I don't know what to contribute it to, what happened-- a miracle from somewhere-- I don't know from where, but it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your brother? Did he keep that job in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: He kept that job, yes. So my mother and I worked there. Then I was fired from there. They needed--

speaker: interviewer
text: From there, meaning the clothes sorting.

speaker: interviewee
text: The clothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorting, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was fired from there. And they made me a super-- they needed superintendents to clean the houses, clean the street, because there, you didn't have toilets in the house. It was outhouses. This had to be cleaned with Clorox every day. No disease should spread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they need superintendent for the home. They made me a superintendent of a certain house, which was-- we were dispossessed from our apartment where we lived.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had an apartment above the floor. In other words, the ground floor was our store. The first floor, we had an apartment with a balcony. But in the ghetto, we were dispossessed from that apartment, because somebody from the ghettos, a big shot, an uncle, needed an apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they say, you can't live here. You're only three people, this apartment's for six. It was an excuse, but a legitimate one at that. And they assigned-- say, since you became a superintendent, we'll give you an apartment to the house you're assigned to, so you wouldn't have to walk there. You'll be in the premises, like, if an inspector comes or needs to check something, you were there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no choice in the matter saying whether I want it or not. That was a fact. So when they took me there, that was a ground floor. It used to be a store-- in the back was a bakery. So the baker who baked the bread at the front of the store, they gave us this store as an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if we had a ground floor, we had windows. So my brother came over, and they said, wait a minute, I can hang up a sign in the window-- watchmaker. And I'll do, after work, after I finish with the [NON-ENGLISH]-- I'll do in the evening, made a few dollars, and we can buy whatever additional food.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother said, it's a very good idea. And in fact, he started teaching me to be a watchmaker. He started off with the big clocks that ring the two, like-- I learned, I remember, I already could take apart a big clock-- not a little watch-- and put it together and clean it. But we had customers. People came in to repair watches. That's all it was, repairs, because we didn't have nothing to sell, so repairs. And we got a little extra food for his money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you leave the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there four years, you said.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, ma'am, four years. One night, middle of the night-- 2 in the morning, 3 in the morning-- the police knocked on the door where we lived. Mother opened up the door, came on, come, we need you, come with us. What, where? We have orders to take you. Fine, I know I didn't commit no crimes. Take me, take me. They took me. They left my mother and my brother. That was in March of 1944. At that time, my brother was already married. He married, in the ghetto, a girl. His wife, which she survived-- she was in Israel, and she remarried somebody else. But anyway, he was already-- so just mother and I were left in that apartment. And they took me out, they took me to a prison, a prison within a prison. In fact, that's a prison that my uncle built. The bricklayer, Uncle Avrumci, he built that. And they didn't get me, alone. They get the-- little did I know, that they went around, the police, and they picked up lots of people-- anyway, quite a few hundred people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came, there, to the prison. So, what happened? Hey, what are you doing? Well, they picked me up, you know. So we saw that they need some people. The Germans ask for people.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there, I would say, about 2 weeks, 14 days, till they gathered up enough what they wanted. And one day, they walked out to trains, and they took us to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother and brother in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. My brother-- oh yes, we were allowed to write from that camp. When we arrived-- I'll come to that-- from that prison, they send us to a camp ammunition factory. We had more food in that camp than we had in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the German, the top guy, the Nazi who was the leader of the whole factory, he said, you are allowed to write a letter to the ghetto. He knew that they were starving there, and here, we had food. We could only write good things, which was a fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wrote a letter to my mother, and we were allowed to get an answer back. One time, a one time deal. My brother died in the ghetto, after I left, of starvation. A young man who was only two years older than I was, he died of starvation.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was alive. Because from the letter that I wrote-- we have very good here. We have food. I can say-- remember, I said four years? That was the day it turned on me with food. I could say, many days, I was not hungry.

speaker: interviewee
text: But again, that wasn't due to what the Germans fed us. But they gave a sufficient diet, like, let me explain. Like, we got 250 grams of bread in the ghetto. They gave out, here, a day, 500 grams. So bread was already double the amount. However, we worked in an ammunition factory with Poles from the outside. And, and, and we worked [INAUDIBLE], so when we left the ghetto, we had clothing. And we could sell-- it was very important, that shirt, slacks-- we used to sell that and get money. For that money, we could buy extra food.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll go to the next tape.
